abstract: This study investigated the influence of different drying techniques on the drying kinetics and final quality of sour cherries (*Prunus cerasus L.*). Specifically, the effects of vacuum–microwave drying (VMD) at varying power levels (initially 240, 360, and 480 W) were compared against convective drying (CD) at 50, 60, and 70 °C, using freeze drying (FD) as a high-quality control. Results indicated that VMD significantly shortened processing times compared to CD, reducing dehydration from several hours to under 50 minutes. While increasing the air temperature in CD and the material temperature in VMD generally led to a degradation of phenolic compounds, antioxidant capacity, and color intensity, VMD proved superior to CD and performed comparably to FD. The highest retention of bioactive compounds and the most favorable color profiles—linked closely to anthocyanin stability—were observed in VMD samples where the microwave power was reduced to 120 W during the final stages of drying to prevent overheating. Notably, the drying process appeared to enhance the extractability of certain quercetin and kaempferol derivatives. These findings suggest that VMD, particularly when power is staged to control internal product temperature, is an efficient and effective method for producing high-quality dried sour cherries with a long shelf life and preserved nutritional value.
